coredata.dc:title: Experimental and theoretical approaches to unveil the interaction mechanisms of carbon dots with 4-nitrophenol
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Cardoso, Alan O.
Bogireddy, Naveen Kumar Reddy
Celaya, Christian A.
Muñiz, Jesús
Agarwal, Vivechana
coredata.dc:description: Developing a non-toxic, cost-effective, biocompatible, selective, and sensitive optical sensor for the optical detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) has been challenging yet significant. Among carbon-based materials, the selectivity of carbon dots toward 4-NP is a key area of research, and a comprehensive understanding is crucial to broaden its practical application. Our unique contribution to this field involves synthesizing and testing different N-doped carbon dots (CDs) for selective 4-NP detection through absorbance and photoluminescence. We have also theoretically provided the interaction mechanism between 4-NP and N-doped CDs (H-NCDs). The optimized hydrothermally synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs demonstrate excellent specificity towards 4-NP through simultaneous quenching of the PL (98 %) and redshift of the signal (34 nm) in an aqueous medium. The density functional theory (DFT) model that was performed to understand the electronic structure properties behind the interaction of 4-NP and CD could be a predictive tool for developing CD-based materials to enhance 4-NP capture. Our study provides a mechanistic understanding of the analyte-specific CD design. It offers promising implications for the practical application of our findings, thereby contributing a unique perspective to the field and benefiting various industries and environmental monitoring efforts, sparking optimism for the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecular sensors
4-nitrophenol
Carbon dots
DFT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and optical characterization of carbon dots
Optimization based on synthesis method, type & concentration of dopant
Optimization of CDs for the detection of 4-NP
Selectivity and sensitivity of 4-NP using optimized H-NCDs
Electronic structure properties of the atomistic models of 4-NP deposited on carbon dots
Band structure and DOS of the composite systems
Bonding mechanism of 4-NP and the carbon models based on AIM calculations
Simulated absorption spectra of 4-NP system supported on the carbon dot models
Possible formation mechanisms of CDs based on the experimental and theoretical evidence
Quenching mechanisms based on the experimental and theoretical evidence

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support given by DGAPA-UNAM (Direcci\u00F3n General de Asuntos del Personal Acad\u00E9mico) under Project No. PAPIIT-(IG100720,IN106122, and IA202324). The authors would like to acknowledge the Supercomputing Department of Universidad Nacional Aut\u00F3noma de M\u00E9xico for the computing resources under Projects No. LANCAD-UNAM-DGTIC-427, LANCAD-UNAM-DGTIC-370 and LANCAD-UNAM-DGTIC-310, and the support given by Fondo Sectorial de Ciencia B\u00E1sica y de Frontera-CONAHCYT under Project No. CBF2023\u20132024-336; and Fronteras de la Ciencia-CONAHCYT under Project No. 21077. Project No. 270810 (Laboratorio Nacional de Conversi\u00F3n y Almacenamiento de Energ\u00EDa-CONACYT) is also acknowledged. C.A.C. wants to acknowledge CONAHCYT-M\u00E9xico for the postdoctoral fellowship. The computing time in an AWS EC2 instance is also acknowledged, which was granted under Project No. 8 of the DGTIC-AWS initiative. The authors are grateful for the technical support on high-performance computing given by Kevin Alquicira Hern\u00E1ndez and H\u00E9ctor Cort\u00E9s Gonz\u00E1lez.

The authors acknowledge the financial support given by DGAPA-UNAM (Direcci\u00F3n General de Asuntos del Personal Acad\u00E9mico) under Project No. PAPIIT-(IG100720, IN106122). Project No. 270810 (Laboratorio Nacional de Conversi\u00F3n y Almacenamiento de Energ\u00EDa-CONACYT) is also acknowledged. The computing time in an AWS EC2 instance is also acknowledged, which was granted under Project No. 8 of the DGTIC-AWS initiative. ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis process of nitrogen-doped carbon dots, their interaction with 4-nitrophenol, and the combination of experimental and theoretical studies to analyze the resulting properties.

Photoluminescence emission spectra (a-c) and absorbance (d-f) spectra of carbon dots as a function of (a) synthesis method,(b) dopant type (using hydrothermal synthesis method) (c) concentration/ proportion of carbon precursor with respect to the dopant (nitrogen) concentration using hydrothermal synthesis method,(d) synthesis method,(e) dopant type (f) dopant concentration.

Bar graph representation (at emission wavelength = 454 nm) obtained from the PL signal (excitation wavelength = 350 nm) of CDs in the presence of 4-NP. Each of the systems was studied by considering (a) changing synthesis method: hydrothermal, pyrolysis, and microwave-assisted,(b) changing dopant type (reference without dopant, N-doped, S-doped, codoped with N and S), (c) changing dopant (Nitrogen) concentration, (d) different proportion of H-NCDs (performed for H-NCDs3). The arrow represents the quantification of the redshift in the presence of 4-NP observed with respect to the reference (pure H-NCDs).(complete PL data given in supplementary information as Fig. S7).

(a) HRTEM images of H-NCDs. (Inset shows the particle size distribution with an average diameter of 3.5 ± 0.3. The estimated value is averaged over 50 particles from different HRTEM images). High resolution, deconvoluted XPS spectra corresponding to H-NCDs (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s. (The intensity ratio is studied before and after the addition of 4-NP to analyze the change in the probe structure) and (d) N1s peaks of H-NCDs.

Optical sensing through H-NCDs (reference) in the presence of 7 different contaminants at 1 mM, (a) selectivity measured through PL signal of the optimized H-NCDs (measured at 350 nm), (b) bar chart of the selectivity through PL (estimated at 454 nm), (c) sensitivity of H-NCDs towards 4-NP (measured at 350 nm), and (d) selectivity observed through absorbance measurements.

Atomistic representation upon geometry optimization of the model systems used in the present work, (a) pristine graphene (1), (b) N-doped model (2), (c) S-doped model (3), and (d) codoped with N and S (4).
Isosurfaces of charge density differences in: (a) pristine graphene (1), (b) nitrogen-doped graphene (2), (c) sulfur-doped graphene (3), and (d) nitrogen/sulfur-doped graphene (4).
AIM analysis for (a) pristine carbon model, (b) nitrogen doped carbon, (c) sulfur doped carbon, and (d) combined nitrogen/sulfur doped-carbon.

Band structure and PDOS profiles of 4-NP interacting with: (a) pristine graphene, (b) N-doped graphene, (c) S-doped graphene, and (d) codoped N and S graphene. The color lines codes for the PDOS profiles correspond to: green/2p-PDOS, gray/H-PDOS, pink/C-PDOS, orange/2s-PDOS, cyan/N-PDOS, blue/O-PDOS, violet/S-PDOS, gold/graphene doped-PDOS, brown/4-NP-PDOS.

Simulated absorption spectra for (a) CD models as a function of photon energy, and (b) CD models as a function of wavelength, (c) CD models in which 4-NP was adsorbed, as a function of photon energy, and (d) CD models in which 4-NP was adsorbed, as a function of wavelength.
Two-dimensional plots of the NCI analysis for systems: (a) pristine graphene, (b) doped with nitrogen, (c) doped with sulfur, and (d) doped with nitrogen and sulfur.
Three-dimensional NCI isosurfaces of (a) pristine graphene, (b) carbon doped with nitrogen, (c) carbon doped with, and (d) carbon doped with nitrogen and sulfur.
Schematic representation of the formation mechanism of CDs.

Proposed photoluminescence quenching mechanism a) Schematic representation of the interaction, b) Normalized optical response. The graph shows four curves: one representing the photoluminescence (PL) of the H-NCDs (black), the absorbance of the 4-nitrophenolate ions (purple), the absorbance (Abs) of the H-NCDs (blue), and the photoluminescence of the H-NCDs with the 4-nitrophenolate ions (yellow).

head.ce:title: Experimental and theoretical approaches to unveil the interaction mechanisms of carbon dots with 4-nitrophenol

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contribute equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing a non-toxic, cost-effective, biocompatible, selective, and sensitive optical sensor for the optical detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) has been challenging yet significant. Among carbon-based materials, the selectivity of carbon dots toward 4-NP is a key area of research, and a comprehensive understanding is crucial to broaden its practical application. Our unique contribution to this field involves synthesizing and testing different N-doped carbon dots (CDs) for selective 4-NP detection through absorbance and photoluminescence. We have also theoretically provided the interaction mechanism between 4-NP and N-doped CDs (H-NCDs). The optimized hydrothermally synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs demonstrate excellent specificity towards 4-NP through simultaneous quenching of the PL (98 %) and redshift of the signal (34 nm) in an aqueous medium. The density functional theory (DFT) model that was performed to understand the electronic structure properties behind the interaction of 4-NP and CD could be a predictive tool for developing CD-based materials to enhance 4-NP capture. Our study provides a mechanistic understanding of the analyte-specific CD design. It offers promising implications for the practical application of our findings, thereby contributing a unique perspective to the field and benefiting various industries and environmental monitoring efforts, sparking optimism for the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Many persistent organic pollutants harm the environment and the health of living beings, even in low concentrations. These pollutants can resist natural degradation caused by the sun, chemical agents, and microorganisms for many years [1,2]. In particular, 4-NP is a pale yellow, sand-like crystalline material among organic pollutants and is used in the manufacturing of fungicides, insecticides, pharmaceuticals and dyes to darken leather [3]. The World Health Organization and Environment Protection Agency listed 4-NP as a highly hazardous chemical with a permissible limit in drinking water of 1 μg/L according to NOM-127-SSA1–1994 [4], as prolonged or high exposure could affect the nervous system and interfere with the ability of the blood to transport oxygen [5]. In addition, breathing 4-NP not only causes respiratory difficulties (irritates the nose, throat, and lungs, causing shortness of breath), it can also cause stomach upset, weakness, rapid heartbeat, confusion, or fever [6] that progresses to collapse and even death [7].

Apart from the degradation of 4-NP using a wide variety of materials/techniques [8], 4-NP identification is usually performed using relatively expensive and complicated techniques (electrochemically or with high performance liquid chromatography) [9,10], in the last few years, there has been interest in developing new portable analytical tools with high selectivity, sensitivity, and fast detection for 4-NP. In this regard, the nanomaterials such as CdTe[11], CdS[12], CdSe [13], gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [14], copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) [15], and graphene oxide/carbon dots (CDs) [1,16] have been extensively used to identify this pollutant. In particular, the detection of 4-NP using fluorescent nanomaterials such as carbon dots involves the quenching of emitted light (when irradiated with electromagnetic radiation [17] of higher energy) in the presence of the contaminant [18]. Moreover, this material is of special interest because of its environmentally friendly and cost-effective nature.

In recent years, CDs (usually smaller than 100 nm) have attracted attention due to their possible formation through green and easily accessible chemical precursors. Highly engaging optical properties, such as high photoluminescence[1,16], indicate that such materials can absorb and emit light at another different wavelength (shorter/longer; up/down conversion) and have been extensively applied for optical applications. This fluorescence can be tuned by controlling the size and composition of the CDs. Regarding electronic properties, CDs may show semiconductor or conductor characteristics depending on their size and structure [19]. This allows CDs to transport electrical charges and be used in electronic and optoelectronic devices. In addition, CDs show a functional surface, indicating that they can be chemically modified to introduce different functional groups [20]. Such a feature permits adjustment of their properties and facilitates their dispersion in different media or binding to other molecules or materials. The practical relevance of these properties is evident in the use of biocompatible and nontoxic CDs in bioimaging [21], catalysis [22], and pollutant detection applications [23].

Researchers have explored various organic precursors, including natural celery leaves, sewage sludge, and ganoderma lucidum to test the selectivity of green CDs against 4-NP. Qu et al. [23] showed the selectivity of CDs derived from natural celery leaves and glutathione (N, S dopants), which were found to be selective to 4-NP, 2-nitrophenol (2-NP) and 3-nitrophenol (3-NP). In another study, Qin et al. [24] investigated Escherichia coli-derived CDs, while Hu and Gao [25] synthesized CDs using sewage sludge that exhibited selectivity towards 4-NP. CDs derived from auricularia auricula (Tu et al. [26]) and ganoderma lucidum spore powder (Tu et al.[27]) were also selective for 4-NP and dinitrophenol. Zhang et al. [28] synthesized CDs using Rosa roxburghii (sweet chestnut rose) as precursors, which showed selectivity towards 2-NP.

Although nitrophenol specific carbon dots have been synthesized chemically as well, using three main dopants (N / S / P) [29–31], no preferred dopant for 4-NP sensing has been intensively studied or established. Instead, the dopant type and concentration choice have been relatively random/arbitrary. In addition, very few combined theoretical-experimental studies have been conducted to understand the mechanism of 4-NP interaction with CDs. For instance, Wang et al. [32] studied the effect of several concentrations of 4-NP, initial concentrations of reductants, catalyst dosages, and reaction temperatures to understand the adsorption and reduction of 4-NP using the computational code Dmol3 [33]. Supong et al. [34] performed a theoretical study at the B3LYP level of theory [35], in which carboxyl groups were found in activated biocarbon and strongly interacted with 4-NP. Furthermore, Bogireddy et al. [36] reported the role of pyridinic N in reducing 4-NP. Due to the importance of the heteroatom studies that have been made on the interaction of 4-NP with the dopant and its relative concentration in N-doped carbon dots (N-CD), advanced computational techniques are strongly required with the aid of theoretical chemistry methods, which allow simulations of molecular models and the corresponding electronic structure properties.

The precise mechanism for understanding why these nanomaterials are effective in sensing 4-NP has not yet been extensively investigated. To understand the mechanism of interaction at the atomic level between CDs and 4-NP, ten different types CD sensing probes (varying dopant types and concentrations using different synthesis techniques) have been tested for the selective identification of 4-NP. After experimental optimization of the dopant type and concentration for 4-NP detection, DFT calculations were performed using four models with different dopants to understand the interaction between differently doped CDs and 4-NP. In particular, Fig. 1 shows a schematic representation of the overall processes performed in this combined experimental/theoretical approach.

In the present study, the following materials were used: citric acid (Fermont, ≥ 99.5 %), thiourea (Fermont, ≥ 99.1 %), urea (Sigma, ≥ 98 %), sodium sulfate (Meyer, ≥ 99 %), 4-nitrophenol, 4-aminophenol, 2-aminophenol, catechol, and phenol (spectrophotometric grade).

CDs were produced by three synthesis techniques, i.e, hydrothermal, microwave- assisted, and pyrolysis, in which citric acid was used as the carbon precursor, thiourea as precursor for the nitrogen/sulfur co-doping, and urea as precursor for doping with nitrogen (the prefix H-, M- and P- symbolize the fabrication technique while defining the sample names). For the hydrothermal synthesis of CDs, the precursors were dissolved in 40 ml of deionized water and treated at 160∘C for 2 h in a stainless steel autoclave with Teflon lining. The detailed methodology is presented in the supplementary information (SI) in section S0.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical characterization of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorbance spectra were measured using a Thermo Fisher Scientific spectrophotometer. A Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer measured the fluorescence emission and excitation spectra. The morphology and particle size distribution were obtained using transmission electron microscopy measurements in a JOEL JEM-ARM200F. The H-CD diffraction pattern was measured using a Bruker D8 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Furthermore, with an ESCA Ulvac-PHI 1600 photoelectron spectrometer, a surface-sensitive quantitative analysis was performed using the Al Kα radiation photon energy (1486.6 ± 0.2 eV).

The selective and sensitive detection of 4-NP was optimized using several carbon dots as fluorescent probes with similar chemical structures. The selectivity of 4-NP was tested among 4-aminophenol, 2-aminophenol, catechol, phenol, 3-nitrophenol and 2-nitrophenol. For testing the selectivity photoluminescence (PL) and absorbance measurements were performed. An amount of 1000μL of reaction mixture containing 980 μL of 1 mM pollutant and 20μL of the CD probe solution was used to evaluate the intensity of the fluorescence signal at an excitation wavelength of λ
ex = 350 nm. For the absorbance measurements, the proportions of CDs and contaminants were the same as used for photoluminescence measurements. All measurements were sampled in triplicate and studied under identical conditions.

The basic structural optimization of CDs and their corresponding electronic properties were studied with DFT in the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) and the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange-correlation functional [37,38]. The calculations were performed using the Quantum ESPRESSO computational code. Projector-augmented wavelet pseudopotentials were used. An energy cut-off of 50 Ry defined the real-space grid for numerical integration. The convergence threshold for the density matrix and the total energy was established at 10−4 eV and 10−5 eV, respectively. Relaxed geometries were obtained with a Brillouin zone k-point mesh of 3 × 3 × 1. Furthermore, the properties of electronic structure related to the density of states (DOS) and band structure were analyzed with a Brillouin zone k-point mesh of 14 × 14 × 1. The semi-empirical Grimme correlation functional DFT-D3 was used to investigate the adsorption of 4-NP on the surface of carbon models used to describe CDs. The electronic structure was analyzed with the non-covalent interactions (NCI) method [39], the electron localization function (ELF) [40], the atoms in molecules (AIM) approach [41], and the density overlap region indicator (DORI) [42]. Such methodologies are described in the SI. In addition, VMD [43] and Multiwfn [44] were also used to examine how the sensor and the analyte interact. This study used the electronic density of the three-dimensional isosurface and two-dimensional slices of the density gradients.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization based on synthesis method, type & concentration of dopant
ce:section.ce:para: The dopant-dependent photoluminescence spectra of CDs exhibit an increase in the following order: N ≫ N-S > S ≈ pristine ( Fig. 2(b)). As presented in Fig. 2(a), the intensities of the PL signal depend on the fabrication technique. The following sequence is observed: hydrothermal (H-NCDs) > pyrolysis (P-NCDs) > microwaves (M-NCDs) (see Fig. 2(a)). The above results confirm that the N-doped CDs obtained with the hydrothermal technique show an optimized high PL signal intensity because of the formation of a uniform particle distribution. Based on these results, an additional set of experiments was performed by changing the amount of dopant; namely, nitrogen (obtained from urea as the precursor) from 0.45 g to 0.05 g (0.45, 0.35, 0.25, 0.15, and 0.05 g), as presented in Fig. 2(c). An increase in the intensities of the PL signal was observed as a consequence of the increase in the concentration of the N-dopant. Moreover, similar responses were found in the absorbance spectra (Fig. 2(d-f)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of CDs for the detection of 4-NP
ce:section.ce:para: The change in the intensity of the PL signal on different CDs (type of dopant, type of synthesis technique, concentration based on the N dopant) in the presence of 4-NP is presented in Fig. 3. N-doped CDs exhibit a more intense PL signal quenching (97 %) with a redshift of 34 nm, compared to other probes such as H-SCDs (58 %) and H-NSCDs (48 %) without a change in signal wavelength. Fig. 3 also shows that N doping in CDs can efficiently improve the selectivity to 4-NP, as previously reported [25,23,26,27]. Furthermore, 4-NP detection is sampled with the N-doped CDs that were fabricated by varying the method. H-NCDs exhibited more PL quenching (97 %) than M-NCDs (83 %) and P-NCDs (76 %). The hydrothermal synthesis that is presented in Fig. 3(a) shows the best selectivity and corresponds to the unique synthesis that redshifts the PL signal, although N was used as a dopant in all cases. This is highly likely because of uniformly sized CDs and a more controlled fabrication process in comparison to microwave and pyrolysis techniques. This can be clearly confirmed from the UV–visible absorbance profiles of the CDs based on the synthesis technique (see Fig. 2(d)). In the sample obtained from the hydrothermal synthesis, the spectrum exhibits a narrower peak. To understand the contribution of dopant concentration in CD during 4-NP detection, selectivity/sensitivity experiments with CD were performed as a function of nitrogen content (achieved by variation in urea concentration) using a hydrothermal technique. These studies show that N-CDs redshift the spectrum as their concentration increases to a similar precursor concentration ( ≈ 1: 1). Fig. 3(d) shows the PL response of 4-NP with different concentrations of nitrogen-doped CDs synthesized via the hydrothermal method, where it can be observed that the optimal concentration is reached at 20 μL (1:49) (As it is the concentration at which the greatest quenching is obtained).

The H-NCD samples analyzed through TEM images (see Fig. 4(a)), show a distribution of sizes ranging from 2 to 5 nm, with an average diameter of 3.5 ± 0.3 nm. The estimated value is averaged over 50 particles from different HRTEM images. XPS analysis is used to identify elements and chemical states in H-NCDs. The H-NCDs scan confirms the presence of characteristic peaks such as O1s, N1s, and C1s at 532, 400, and 285 eV, respectively. In addition, the high-resolution XPS C1s, O1s, and N1s peaks were deconvoluted to understand their chemistry. The deconvolution spectra of C1s (see Fig. 4(b)) exhibited the existence of C-C (284.3 eV), C-O (285.6 eV), CO (287.1 eV), and O-CO (288.4 eV). This evidences the presence of carbonyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of H-NCDs. Moreover, the C-N contribution (288.4 eV) indicates that the N atoms have been successfully incorporated into the structure of H-NCDs. The deconvoluted high-resolution spectra of O1 and N1 of H-NCDs also confirm the existence of CO (531.7 eV) and C-O (533.3 eV) bonding pairs. In addition, pyridinic N (399.6 eV) and graphitic N/amine N are evidenced, as shown in Fig. 4(c) and (d)). These results indicate the existence of functional groups that contain oxygen. In addition, the presence of N atoms on the surface of the H-NCDs was also found. Furthermore, the CDs, with their lattice spacings of 0.221 nm for G (100), 0.110 nm for G (020), and 0.203 nm and 0.129 nm for diamond-like phases (111 and 110, respectively, as shown in Fig. S6), were identified. These values align perfectly with the characteristics of graphitic [(100), (020)] and diamond-like [(111), (110)] structures [1].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of 4-NP using optimized H-NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: As observed in the previous results, the carbon dots with the best sensing properties are H-NCDs. Therefore, we studied their optical response to different contaminants (4-NP among 4-aminophenol, 2-aminophenol, phenol, and catechol), as shown in Fig. 5(a-b) and (d). H-NCDs are highly selective to 4-NP, as they present the lowest intensity of the PL signal accompanied by a redshift (from 454 to 488 nm), as presented in Fig. 5(a) and a higher absorbance at 410 nm (see Fig. 5(d)). The characteristic peak of absorbance at 410 nm corresponds to the formation of the 4-nitrophenolate ion, since the pH of the N-CD synthesized by the hydrothermal method is around 8. It is well known that 4-NP can easily convert from 4-NP to 4-nitrophenolate ion under alkaline conditions [1]. Furthermore, it confirms that the 4-NP molecule is converting to a 4-nitrophenolate ion before interacting with H-NCDs (detailed information is presented below). In addition, Fig. 5(c) presents the fluorescence spectra of nitrogen-doped CDs (spiked with 4-NP) as a function of 4-NP concentration (ranging from 0.5 to 1000 μM). The precision of our measurements is evident in the decrease in the PL intensity with the increase in 4-NP concentration, a change accompanied by a redshift towards longer wavelengths. This change is detectable with PL spectroscopy even at the low 0.5 μM concentration and the estimated quantum yield for the H-NCDs was ≈ 12.17 % [45]. Moreover, as a comparative study, the 2-NP and 3-NP molecules were also tested to confirm the selectivity of the proposed nanoprobes (H-NCDs) towards 4-NP. As can be observed in the photoluminescence measurements (Fig. 5(a)), 3-NP and 2-NP do not cause quenching or a redshift as evident as in the case of 4-NP. On the other hand, apart from the other analytes, Fig. 5(d) shows that the absorbance spectra of 2-NP and 3-NP overlap with the reference spectrum (i.e., pure H-NCDs), with the only noticeable change being caused by 4-NP, being the only molecule with a peak at 400 nm (characteristic of 4-nitrophenolate ions).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic structure properties of the atomistic models of 4-NP deposited on carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectra depicted in Fig. 5 showed the behavior of 4-NP adsorption on the different carbon materials under study. In order to shed light on the atomistic mechanisms that give rise to such phenomena, theoretical calculations on the electronic structure properties of such materials were performed. A monomer of the 4-NP system (see Fig. 6) was used as the molecular model to interact with carbon-based materials. As previously known [46], the microstructure of carbon firmly preserves a graphitic geometry that theoretically allows us to model carbon-derived systems at an affordable computational cost. In this regard, a graphene model was adopted to simulate the superficial structure of CD throughout this work and to understand the interfacial interaction with 4-NP. It is important to note that such models maintain periodic conditions (as given in the computational details section). Fig. S2(a) of SI shows a top view of the graphene supercell implemented to perform the calculations, while Fig. S2(b)-(c) show the relaxed structures subjected to nitrogen and sulfur doping, respectively. Fig. S2(d) exhibits combined doping in which both N and S are adsorbed.

N-doped graphene simulates the carbon dot experimentally subjected to the influence of urea, since the XPS results exhibited the N-doping on the surface of the carbon dots (see Fig. 4). The system in Fig. S2(c) of SI shows the carbon dot model in the presence of S (experimentally treated with sodium thiosulfate). The expected mixed doping in the carbon dots under the influence of thiourea is given in Fig. S2(d) of SI after a geometry relaxation. Fig. 6(a)-(d) summarizes the geometry optimizations where 4-NP interacts with each of the model systems under study. The top and lateral views show the locations in the carbon models where the 4-NP is preferentially adsorbed. The adsorption energies for all systems were calculated via the Eq. 1 (shown in the SI). The values of such energies are presented in Table S1 of the SI. The distances between the carbon models and the closest atom of the 4-NP molecule are given in Fig. 6. The magnitude of the adsorption energies indicates that a weak electrostatic attraction, presumably of the van der Waals (vdW) type, rules the interaction among both systems. In addition, the interplanar distances suggest the same assumption. However, adsorption on the S-doped system is enhanced (see Table S2) with an interaction distance of 0.0408 Å, which could be addressed to a covalent-type bonding. In order to understand the electron transfer mechanisms behind the interaction of the 4-NP molecule and the carbon-derived materials, the density difference isosurfaces were mapped according to Eq. 2, in the Computational details section of SI, in which the regions colored in blue (see Fig. 7(a)) denote the locations where the electrons were depleted, and the yellow regions indicate the locations where the electrons were transferred. As expected previously, the interfacial volume in system 2 exhibits a significant contribution (see Fig. 7(b)) that can be understood with the aid of the two-dimensional slices depicted in the insets. The red regions represent an electron excess that can be interpreted as a screening agent that gives rise to the covalent-type interaction previously suggested. Nonetheless, systems 1, 3, and 4 show no electron density distribution at the interface, which is also absent in the two-dimensional slices of the insets in Fig. 7(a), (c) and (d). This behavior initially suggests that a weak or no interaction is expected in each system. In this respect, it is strictly necessary to study the phenomenology of interatomic interactions via the descriptor analysis of electronic structure properties. That is in order to deepen into the origin of the possible attraction among the 4-NP molecule with the pristine and doped carbon layers, ELF and DORI computations were additionally performed (shown in Fig. S4). With the ELF analysis, it is possible to elucidate the electronic charge distribution at the volume between the 4-NP and the carbon models under study. The S-doped graphene shows a significant contribution of the ELF at the interface. This represents a further probe for the presumable covalent-type nature of the interaction, as the enhanced delocalization at the region between both structures exhibits it. Nevertheless, the ELF distribution for systems 1, 3, and 4 are similar, showing no delocalization at the interface. Also, the adsorption energy calculations show that the interaction of 2-nitrophenol (2-NP) and 3-nitrophenol (3-NP) with the CDs is electrostatic, as their adsorption energy is 0.04 eV. The relaxation geometries are presented in Fig. S5. On the other hand, when comparing their adsorption energies, a clear and logical progression is observed: 4-nitrophenolate ion (4.659 eV) > 2-NP (0.049 eV) > 3-NP (0.043 eV). Similarly, when comparing the quenching (estimated at 454 nm) of the photoluminescence spectra obtained experimentally (Fig. 5), the order is 4-nitrophenolate ion (97.3 %) > 2-NP (76.11 %) > 3-NP (36.85 %), which is in agreement with the theoretical calculations. This behavior is evidenced by the interaction distance (greater than 4.0 Å) between the adsorbate molecules (2-NP and 3-NP) and the N-doped graphene surface (see Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band structure and DOS of the composite systems
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S3(a)-(d) exhibits the DOS and band structures of systems 1-4. The DOS of graphene presents a typical non-semiconducting nature in which the band structure also shows the Dirac cone at the Γ-point. This indicates an adequate modeling of the graphene system. The N-doped graphene model reveals a precise modification around the Fermi level, in which the rise of novel states is formed (see Fig. S3(b)). It also induces the formation of bands that cross the conduction band and break the original symmetry of pristine graphene. An analogous behavior is also given for S-doped graphene, and an enhanced number of states is raised. This behavior addresses a conductive-like character that could trigger an enhanced volume of delocalized electrons, which could interact with a different electronic distribution, such as that of the 4-NP molecule.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bonding mechanism of 4-NP and the carbon models based on AIM calculations
ce:section.ce:para: In order to gain insight into the nature of the bonding between 4-NP contaminant, which is adsorbed onto the carbon-based models, atoms in molecules (AIM) were performed according to the methodology given in the computational details section of SI. The criteria of the AIM methodology allow us to locate bond critical points (BCP) in the region between the 4-NP molecule and the graphene-derived models. The BCP is used to identify the presence of an interaction that could be ruled by a covalent, ionic, or hydrogen bonding. Fig. 8(a)-(d) show the geometrical locations of such points. Table S2 of SI revealed that S-doped graphene induces a strong attraction with BCP close to the 4-NP system. The interaction corresponds to a covalent bond that caused the breaking of the 4-NP planarity. Furthermore, the effect of the interaction induced the break of the N-doped graphene planarity and the detachment of the C atoms under the 4-NP molecule. Otherwise, the interaction with the S-doped and N/S-doped graphene preserves the planarity of the organic molecule. In both cases, the carbon models virtually kept the original planarity, with the characteristic deviation of the S atom (see Fig. 8 (c) and (d)). According to the AIM calculations (Table S2 of SI), the origin of the interaction was ascribed to a weak hydrogen bond. It should be mentioned that the AIM values also amounted to an H-bonding interaction for the graphene and 4-NP molecules.

As a further analysis to evidence the involvement of the 4-NP molecule with the carbon dot models, the DOS/PDOS and the electronic band structures of the carbon models in which 4-NP was allowed to interact are presented in Fig. 9. As previously suggested by the AIM and DORI calculations, an electrostatic interaction of the vdW-type rules the attraction among the 4-NP system and the carbon models. This behavior was also found in the DOS shown in Fig. 9(a)-(d), in which the PDOS coming from the 4-NP are evidenced with the notation given in the captions in Fig. 9. The PDOS revealed an overlap of the electrostatic states in 4-NP with those of the four carbon models (a-d). The overlap could be directly attributed to the weak interaction of the organic molecule with the pristine graphene and each of the doped-carbon models under study. Furthermore, the significant contribution to the change in the conduction states is given by the oxygen and nitrogen present in 4-NP, underscoring the depth of our study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulated absorption spectra of 4-NP system supported on the carbon dot models
ce:section.ce:para: The time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) methodology [47] is presented in the Computational details section of SI. TDDFT was used to simulate the absorption spectra. Fig. 10 depicts the absorption spectra of the four carbon models under study. The absorption is subjected to the following trend GP > GP-N > GP-NS > GP-S, indicating that the absorption is significant for pristine graphene. As previously stated, the GP-S system presents the most considerable adsorption energy of all models under study, which may be addressed to the capability to retain a sulfur atom with prominent strength. Consequently, this could give the poorest availability for absorbing light because of the enhanced interaction of the carbon layer with sulfur. Because the adsorption energy is smaller for the other doped systems, the absorption appeared more effective but weaker than that of pristine graphene. This behavior is in close agreement with the experimental results exhibited in Fig. 2(e), in which the sulfur-doped CDs show the smallest absorption profile of the systems under study. This theoretical evidence supports the use of the simplified model systems considered throughout the present work. In order to model the adsorption of the 4-NP contaminant agent on the different carbon dot models, additional TDDFT calculations were performed from the relaxed structures in which 4-NP interacts with carbon, as previously evidenced in the discussion in section 3. Fig. 10(c) exhibits one of the smallest amplitudes in the maxima of the simulated absorption spectra. Moreover, the tail (black curve) is extended in a considerable interval of the given wavelength window under the other absorption spectra of the studied systems. This is in excellent agreement with the experiment since Fig. 2(e) reveals that N-CD is the system with the lowest absorption coefficient. Moreover, the simulated spectra show that the absorption behavior is very similar throughout all wavelength intervals for the graphene S-doped (GP-S) and graphene N-doped (GP-NS) systems. Note that such a character is extended up to the end of the interval, but it is superior to the absorption given for the GP-N system. The 4-NP molecule adsorbed on the pristine graphene model shows the most significant maximum from all series. However, this behavior is not constant throughout the entire wavelength range. The tail remains at intermediate values between the GP-N model and those of GP-S/GP-NS (see Fig. 10 (c) and (d)), which is in close agreement with the experimental observation in which an intermediate absorption coefficient was reported, lying between the bare CD and the S-CD and NS-CD systems.

Consequently, the theoretical analysis shows the potential to be applied practically in selecting the optimal doped CD material that can efficiently capture a contaminant molecule. This theoretical procedure, if implemented, could serve as a valuable tool to tailor the carbon material before the experimental treatment, thereby enhancing the efficiency of the treatment process.

The primary interaction between the sensor and 4-NP is non-covalent, as previously described (Figs. 7 and S5 of SI). To further understand the type of interaction, we conducted an NCI analysis. It provides a visual representation of the attractive and repulsive forces in the pristine and doped systems, using a two-dimensional plot and a three-dimensional isosurface. On the isosurface, color contours vividly indicate the type of interaction: green zones represent weak interactions, such as London dispersion forces; blue regions indicate strong interactions, including dipole-dipole attractions, and the red zones exhibit steric repulsions. A noticeable contribution is exhibited (see Fig. 12) on the three-dimensional isosurface between the aromatic rings of all systems that interact with 4-NP (except the system that interacts with the 4-nitrophenolate ion). A π-π interaction rules this attraction. The exception with the 4-nitrophenolate is due to a non-covalent bonding, which corresponds to an ionic bonding instead.

The two-dimensional NCI was plotted with the (λ 2)ρ sign function on the x-axis and the reduced density gradient (RDG) on the y-axis. In the two-dimensional NCI plot (see Fig. 11), the green peaks between 0.00 and − 0.02 represent a weak NCI interaction; namely, the π-π attraction, while the red peaks indicated repulsive forces. The NCI analysis, with its high accuracy, is in close agreement with the experimental outcomes Fig. 12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible formation mechanisms of CDs based on the experimental and theoretical evidence
ce:section.ce:para: The light emitted by the optimized H-NCDs is predominantly blue, with a peak around 440–450 nm. This emission is attributed mainly to quantum confinement effects in nanostructures with comparable dimensions to the exciton Bohr radius (as shown in the HRTEM image; Fig. 4) [48–50]. Quantum confinement significantly modifies the electronic and optical properties of the particles, leading to discrete energy levels rather than the continuous bands observed in bulk materials. As a result, electronic transitions within these quantized energy levels can produce light at specific wavelengths, contributing to the blue photoluminescence observed in CDs. The PL of CDs arises from radiative transitions between excited and ground electronic states. Various factors can influence these transitions, including the size and shape of the CDs, the surface functionalization, and the presence of heteroatoms or dopants. In particular, the role of interactions between carbon atoms and the functional groups or dopants on the surface in creating localized states that enhance light emission in the blue spectral range is crucial (Fig. 9).

Surface passivation and the presence of oxygen-containing groups, such as carboxyl or hydroxyl groups, play a significant role in altering the surface states and influencing photoluminescence. These effects, as evidenced through XPS analysis (Fig. 4(b-d)), can facilitate non-radiative or radiative recombination pathways, thereby shifting the emission wavelength and influencing the intensity of the emitted light [51]. The interplay between core-state emission, related to quantum confinement, and surface-state emission, related to surface chemistry, is critical in determining the overall photoluminescence characteristics of the CDs ( Fig. 13).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanisms based on the experimental and theoretical evidence
ce:section.ce:para: The present work proposes a two-step PL quenching mechanism during the CDs’ interaction with 4-NP. This thoroughly investigated mechanism begins with the CD solution at pH 8 (no alteration in pH). When the 4-NP comes into contact with the solution of CDs, it deprotonates and forms a 4-nitrophenolate ion without CD interaction ( Fig. 14(a)). The normalized absorbance spectrum of the 4-nitrophenolate ion show an overlapping region with the absorbance and emission spectra of the CDs (Fig. 14(b)), explaining the quenching partially due to an Inner Filter Effect (IFE) [52,53]. In addition, the CDs are found to interact with the 4-nitrophenolate ions, that is revealed through a PL signal redshift attributed to the possible electron transfer from the amine groups of CDs to the 4-nitrophenolate ions. The NCI calculations (Fig. 12(b)) show the presence of a covalent bond between the surface of the CDs and the 4-nitrophenolate ions.

An experimental and theoretical study was performed to elucidate the origin of the adsorption of contaminants, such as 4-NP in carbon dots. Close agreement was evidenced between the theoretical and experimental results, as the same trend was observed between the selectivity of 4-NP that was experimentally measured through absorbance and photoluminescence, and the theoretical results obtained via the adsorption energy calculations on each type of bonding (via AIM, DORI, ELF and NCI). That is, the largest adsorption energy computed for the theoretical model corresponds to the carbon system doped with N, which evidences the enhanced retention. Moreover, the increase in the absorption profile for the doped CD with N is also in agreement with the theoretical TDDFT results. This reveals its improved affinity for absorption of light radiation. This behavior may be extended to low-dimensional materials that aim to capture 4-NP. Furthermore, theoretical models indicate that the addition of dopants increases the adsorption of the organic contaminant. As was exhibited in all doped systems, the adsorption energy was larger as compared to the pristine system. The band structure and PDOS showed that the selectivity to 4-NP is due to its nitro (-NO2) functional group. This is evidenced by the presence of 4-NP contributions in the conduction band. The combined experimental/theoretical methodology presented in this work may be used as a tool to tailor sensor materials to capture molecular adverse agents that affect human health.

The capture of contaminant molecules by using materials whose production represents a green process is a subject of intense research. In this contribution, we present the green synthesis of carbon dot (CD)-based materials which are intended to selectively capture 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). This molecule represents a highly hazardous contaminant that is required to be identified and subsequently immobilized. In this regard, the optimized hydrothermally synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs showed excellent specificity toward 4-NP through simultaneous quenching of the PL (98 %) and redshift of the signal (34 nm) in an aqueous medium.

Christian A. Celaya: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Naveen Kumar Reddy Bogireddy: Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Alan O. Cardoso: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jesús Muñiz: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Investigation. Vivechana Agarwal: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support given by DGAPA-UNAM (Dirección General de Asuntos del Personal Académico) under Project No. PAPIIT-(IG100720,IN106122, and IA202324). The authors would like to acknowledge the Supercomputing Department of Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México for the computing resources under Projects No. LANCAD-UNAM-DGTIC-427, LANCAD-UNAM-DGTIC-370 and LANCAD-UNAM-DGTIC-310, and the support given by Fondo Sectorial de Ciencia Básica y de Frontera-CONAHCYT under Project No. CBF2023–2024-336; and Fronteras de la Ciencia-CONAHCYT under Project No. 21077. Project No. 270810 (Laboratorio Nacional de Conversión y Almacenamiento de Energía-CONACYT) is also acknowledged. C.A.C. wants to acknowledge CONAHCYT-México for the postdoctoral fellowship. The computing time in an AWS EC2 instance is also acknowledged, which was granted under Project No. 8 of the DGTIC-AWS initiative. The authors are grateful for the technical support on high-performance computing given by Kevin Alquicira Hernández and Héctor Cortés González.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2024.136783.

ce:section.ce:para:

.
